unit.name: Plain autoencoders and the motivation for VAEs
unit.description: Builds intuition for VAEs by first showing why a bottlenecked autoencoder learns features but does not by itself solve generation.
unit.summary: The instructor temporarily drops the 'variational' part and first reviews standard autoencoders as an unsupervised way to learn features z from data x without labels [ts=2904s] [ts=2916s]. The basic training recipe is to encode x into a latent vector z, decode z back to x, and optimize reconstruction; the point is not to learn the identity function itself but to force useful structure through a narrow bottleneck so that z is far smaller than the original image [ts=2963s] [ts=2992s] [ts=3022s]. After training, that bottleneck representation can be reused for downstream tasks in the same spirit as other self-supervised methods [ts=3059s] [ts=3064s]. However, generation remains unsolved: sampling a z that matches the learned latent distribution is no easier than sampling x directly, so a plain autoencoder does not yet give a principled generative model [ts=3077s] [ts=3096s] [ts=3130s]. The VAE idea is to impose a known probabilistic structure, typically Gaussian, on the latent space so that after training one can sample z from that known distribution and decode it into new data [ts=3134s] [ts=3153s] [ts=3171s].

key_points.text: A plain autoencoder learns features z from x by reconstructing the input without labels.
Reconstruction is only useful when the latent code z is bottlenecked so the model must compress structure from the data.
The autoencoder's latent code can be reused as a self-supervised representation for downstream tasks.
Plain autoencoders do not solve generation because sampling z that matches the data-induced latent distribution is still hard.
VAEs address this by forcing the latent codes to follow a known distribution such as a Gaussian.
